topic: Trade in the Contemporary World / Global Migration / Human Rights / Power & Borders
subject: geography

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.
It's McLean. “Bezos” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does the KOF Index measure?
items.why: Correct answer: Globalisation. “Poverty” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Top 500 TNCs control roughly what percentage of trade?
items.why: Exact answer: 70 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
